nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rubble areas on steep outer reef slopes, recorded at depths of about 8–70 m; usually solitary or in small groups of four to five individuals

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of enriched frozen brine and mysis shrimp alongside spirulina, marine algae, and a quality prepared marine angelfish formula. Feeding 2–3 times per day is commonly advised to help discourage nipping at corals and clam mantles.

summary: The Joculator Angelfish is a rare and vividly colored dwarf angelfish found only around Christmas Island and the Cocos (Keeling) Islands. Its striking yellow-and-blue coloration and limited range make it one of the more sought-after Centropyge species, though it tends to be semi-aggressive and is best kept singly.
careNotes: The Joculator Angelfish is endemic to a small area of the eastern Indian Ocean — specifically the waters around Christmas Island and the Cocos (Keeling) Islands — which is a key reason it is rarely seen and commands a high price when available. It is generally considered hardy once established in a well-maintained aquarium, but newcomers should plan to add it last to a peaceful community, allowing it to settle in without the added stress of defending against newly arrived tankmates. Plenty of live rock for grazing and shelter is important, alongside open water for swimming. The species is widely reported to be intolerant of conspecifics and tends to be aggressive toward other angelfish with a similar body shape, such as the Bicolor and Rock Beauty — it is best kept singly unless a confirmed male/female pair is introduced simultaneously in a very large system. Reef compatibility comes with caution: individuals have been known to nip at stony coral polyps (the small fleshy animals that make up hard corals), soft corals, and the fleshy mantles of clams. Feeding two to three times daily is generally advised to help reduce this nipping behavior. When netting this species, handle carefully — like all angelfish, it has sharp cheek spines (called preopercular spines) that can catch in mesh.
breedingNotes: Centropyge joculator is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals begin life as female and the dominant fish in a group transitions to male. In the wild, it forms harems in which a single male presides over up to four females; if the male is lost, the dominant female generally transitions to take his place. Like other dwarf angelfish, it spawns at dusk by releasing eggs and sperm into open water with no parental care — a strategy known as broadcast or pelagic spawning. Captive breeding has been reported only sporadically by specialist breeders and is considered very difficult outside a professional setting.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used to confirm protogynous hermaphroditism and haremic social structure (one male with up to four females).

fish.breedingNotes: A protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems in the wild, with the dominant female transitioning to male if the male is lost. Spawning occurs at dusk via broadcast release of eggs and sperm into open water. Captive rearing of larvae has been reported only by specialist breeders and is not achievable at a typical hobbyist level.
